nodes.title: Fail fast when lengths differ
nodes.why: Arrays with different lengths can never be equal, so we can stop early.
nodes.alternative: Skip this check and rely on element-by-element comparison only.
nodes.tradeoff: Cheap early exit and clearer intent, with one extra condition line.

nodes.title: Compare each index directly
nodes.why: A plain loop makes the comparison logic explicit and easy to debug.
nodes.alternative: Use `every()` to express the same rule declaratively.
nodes.tradeoff: Very readable control flow, but slightly more verbose than helper methods.

nodes.title: Use strict equality for element checks
nodes.why: Strict comparison avoids coercion surprises like `1 == '1'`.
nodes.alternative: Use loose equality (`!=`) and allow type coercion.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer for interview expectations, but treats numerically equivalent different types as unequal.

nodes.title: Combine length check with `every`
nodes.why: The right side runs only when lengths match, so we avoid unnecessary comparisons.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact one-liner, but denser than step-by-step flow.

nodes.title: Use `every` to enforce all comparisons
nodes.why: `every` returns true only if each element matches at the same index.
nodes.alternative: Manual `for` loop with early return on mismatch.
nodes.tradeoff: Declarative and concise, though callback syntax can feel less direct to beginners.

nodes.title: Use callback index to compare counterpart
nodes.why: The callback index naturally maps each item in `a` to the same position in `b`.
nodes.alternative: Zip arrays first, then compare pair objects.
nodes.tradeoff: Minimal overhead, but assumes index-based equality semantics.

nodes.title: Serialize both arrays to strings
nodes.why: Converting arrays to JSON creates a quick string form for direct comparison.
nodes.alternative: Compare values directly with loop or `every`.
nodes.tradeoff: Very short code, but correctness depends on data shape and serialization rules.

nodes.title: Compare serialized outputs directly
nodes.why: If serialized forms are identical, values and order usually match for simple arrays.
nodes.alternative: Deep comparison logic aware of types and edge cases.
nodes.tradeoff: Handy shortcut, but can break for `undefined`, functions, or non-JSON-safe values.

nodes.title: Treat this as a shortcut, not default strategy
nodes.why: It is useful for quick prototypes but not robust for complex real-world inputs.
nodes.alternative: Use explicit equality logic tailored to expected input types.
nodes.tradeoff: Fast to write, but easier to misuse in production scenarios.
